title: TBM 398: Micromanagement? Or Staying Close to Reality?
questions: At what point does staying close to frontline reality become micromanagement?
How can leaders maintain direct line of sight without undermining empowerment?
What organizational conditions are required for mission-command-like exposure to work safely?
Why do MBO-style reporting rituals often replace judgment and early escalation?
search_terms: management by objectives mbo vs mission command
summary: The post explores why lightweight, frequent senior-leader reporting from many teams can trigger micromanagement fears, even when the intent is respect and useful context. It contrasts Drucker-style MBO with mission command to argue that the difference is often in how organizations operationalize intent, judgment, escalation, and feedback. It’s aimed at leaders and tech org designers trying to build mechanisms that let leaders stay close to messy reality without harming empowerment.

title: TBM 366: Empowered For Delivery, But...
questions: How can a company empower teams while still preventing chaos and protecting executive focus?
Why do dissent and feedback get routed through narrow or ineffective channels in “autonomous” organizations?
What conditions make mission command work, and what happens when those conditions are missing?
What should individuals do when their company’s culture conflicts with their values about autonomy and feedback?
search_terms: manager as gatekeeper filter escalation resolver
empowered teams but don't challenge strategy
summary: The post critiques how many tech organizations claim to practice empowerment and autonomy while still operating with managerial gatekeeping and tightly controlled escalation. It argues that mission command requires trust, shared intent, and safe dissent infrastructure—conditions often missing in low-trust corporate environments. It is aimed at leaders and practitioners trying to navigate or improve feedback, escalation, and autonomy in modern organizations.

title: TBM 330: (Un)common Sense Playbook
questions: How can an engineering manager stabilize a struggling team without losing momentum for experimentation?
What enables a commitment pause or “time out” to succeed with stakeholders and the team?
Why do otherwise competent managers struggle to get buy-in on basic changes?
When should the problem be framed as environment and conditions rather than individual manager skill?
search_terms: how to stabilize a team temporary pause
time out social capital stakeholder management
trust and psychological safety after layoffs
summary: The post outlines a “common sense” engineering management playbook for turning around struggling teams: align on the why, engage customers, stabilize via a temporary pause, set metrics and cadence, and gradually reintroduce work. It’s aimed at engineering leaders and managers who want to understand why these tactics sometimes fail despite being straightforward. The author argues that execution depends heavily on environmental conditions—optics pressure, toxic influence, trust, and partner-team capacity—not just manager skill.

title: TBM 318: Why Orgs Become Too Tall
questions: Why do companies end up with excessive management layers instead of staying flatter?
What prevents managers from increasing spans of control as teams grow?
How do functional vs business unit structures affect coordination costs and org height?
What operational and cultural factors (WIP, interrupts, trust) drive the need for more layers?
summary: This post explains why organizations become “too tall” by mapping the many drivers of added layers and reduced spans of control. It’s for leaders and org-design practitioners who are considering flattening efforts and want a holistic diagnostic of structural, operational, and cultural causes rather than a single simplistic rationale.

title: TBM 316: Ultra Founder Mode
questions: What does radical ownership look like in day-to-day leadership behavior?
How should founders respond to brutal feedback without blaming others?
When should a leader stop delegating and personally own critical problems?
How do you decide what to kill (ideas, metrics, acquisitions) versus what to double down on?
search_terms: radical ownership no we no blame
founder hands-on sales product customer immersion
summary: The post is a maximalist, satirical manifesto for an “Ultra Founder Mode” mindset that rejects comfort and demands extreme personal accountability. It’s aimed at founders and startup leaders who want a high-intensity framework for handling brutal feedback, owning failures, staying hands-on across execution, and cutting initiatives that don’t hit metrics.

title: TBM 296: Trust Lets You Observe Reality
questions: Why can’t leaders get a straight answer about what’s really going wrong?
How do low-trust environments turn metrics into targets that people game?
What should leaders do when they suspect teams are underperforming but trust is low?
How can psychological safety change whether data reflects reality?
search_terms: how to surface reality in organizations
summary: This post argues that in dysfunctional, low-trust organizations, attempts to “surface reality” through objectivity and simplified metrics often fail and can worsen the situation. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who are trying to diagnose organizational problems, set performance measures, and improve feedback and accountability without falling into metric gaming or blame-driven dynamics.

title: TBM 295: Going Deep 🤮
questions: When leaders say 'go deep,' are they asking for real problem-solving or just deflecting responsibility?
How do organizational complexity and dependencies limit a manager’s ability to get into the details?
What does effective delegation look like when teams can’t act independently?
How should managers respond when systemic issues are dismissed as 'not going deep'?
summary: This post argues that 'going deep' and 'get into the details' are often used as leadership clichés that gaslight managers by blaming them for systemic constraints. It’s aimed at engineering managers and leaders who want to understand how organizational complexity, dependencies, and delegation failures make 'going deep' unrealistic, and how to push back on blame-shifting narratives.

title: TBM 269: Three Organizational Design Principles
questions: How can we design organizations so leadership collaboration matches the collaboration required at the front line?
What alignment gaps prevent teams from succeeding even when everyone is trying hard?
What happens when leadership avoids the 'elephants in the room' and teams must improvise?
How does Ashby’s law apply to organizational design and managerial decision-making?
search_terms: alignment gaps leaders vs front line
elephants in the room leadership avoidance
Ashby's law of requisite variety in organizations
human bandwidth limits for trust and teams
summary: The post proposes three organizational design principles for improving collaboration between leaders/managers and front-line teams, arguing that leadership alignment and collaboration must be at least as strong as what the work requires. It frames organizational failure modes as alignment gaps and leadership avoidance of “elephants in the room,” which push teams into fragile pragmatism. It is aimed at leaders and managers designing org structures and operating models for complex work.

title: TBM 268: "Going Deep"
questions: How can leaders know teams are doing a good job without understanding the details?
Why does “going deep” become necessary when companies shift from growth to frugality?
How do fear and blame lead to defensive opacity between executives and front lines?
What organizational changes improve information flow and decision quality without creating a culture of fear?
search_terms: leaders need to know the details
summary: The post argues that “going deep” is a necessary leadership response to orgs that became too stretched and detail-detached during rapid growth. It’s aimed at executives and senior leaders trying to improve decision-making and team advocacy during tougher times without escalating fear, blame, or defensive opacity. The proposed remedy focuses on restoring fundamentals like information flow and creating periodic, respectful direct interactions between teams and senior leaders.

title: TBM 265: Rebuilding Trust and Breaking Free From Trust Proxies and The Swirl
questions: How do we tell whether what we’re debating is a proxy for a deeper trust issue?
What causes trust spirals between a few senior leaders to paralyze an entire organization?
How can leaders improve information flow so reality can be discussed instead of politicized stand-offs?
What practical steps help teams rebuild trust without scapegoating individuals?
summary: This post explores how organizational trust problems—especially between senior leaders—create a self-reinforcing cycle where teams and leaders rely on “trust proxies” instead of addressing underlying issues. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who need practical ways to break the distrust spiral by improving information flow, labeling proxy behaviors, taking responsibility, and reframing “trust” as “tension.”

title: TBM 258: "Just Hire Talented People and Empower Them"
questions: When does 'hire smart people and get out of the way' actually work?
Why does empowerment break down in larger, more complex organizations?
How should leaders change their approach to oversight as the company scales?
What organizational mechanisms prevent local optimization and information degradation?
summary: The post challenges the Silicon Valley leadership mantra that great leaders simply hire talented people and empower them to execute, arguing it is highly context-dependent. It explains why the approach fails as organizations grow due to coordination complexity, incentive conflicts, and information degradation, and it reframes leadership at scale as organizational design—clarity, coherent strategy, and systems that handle recurring problems. The intended audience is leaders and operators thinking about how to manage execution and empowerment across different company sizes.

title: TBM 245: True Empowerment
questions: Are you stepping up or stepping back when challenges arise?
When things go wrong, do you solve the problem or shift blame?
Are you truly empowering your team or just handing off tasks you don’t want?
Do you support your team differently during setbacks than during success?
search_terms: leadership accountability when things go wrong
how to delegate without avoiding responsibility
summary: The post argues that real empowerment is not a leadership slogan or task handoff, but a practice of providing resources and support while maintaining accountability for outcomes. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who want to delegate effectively and build trust, especially when decisions fail or pressure rises. It challenges readers to examine their behavior during setbacks to determine whether they are empowering teams or avoiding responsibility.

title: TBM 239: "Get Creative"
questions: How can I ask for creative solutions without sounding like I’m dumping responsibility on the team?
What should I say instead of “get creative” when resources are constrained?
How do I communicate constraints and still motivate the team to explore options?
How does my framing change how teams interpret my intent?
search_terms: how to ask for creative ideas under constraints
summary: The post helps leaders improve how they communicate when asking teams to find creative solutions under constraints. It explains why “get creative” is frequently misheard as a lack of support and an offloading of responsibility, and offers a more effective framing that names constraints, makes a specific request, and signals shared ownership. It’s aimed at managers and team leads who want better alignment and motivation during tense, high-stakes situations.

title: TBM 223: Why Aren't They Saying Anything?
questions: Why aren’t employees speaking up even when something seems broken?
How can leaders design feedback channels so people know how and where to raise issues, including those involving managers?
What prevents people from connecting symptoms like cross-team communication problems to root causes in org design?
Is relying on surveys or telling people to speak to their managers enough to surface real problems?
search_terms: how to encourage speaking up at work
how to raise issues with your manager
summary: The post explores why leaders often hear little about problems even when many employees notice them, arguing that silence is driven by multiple behavioral and organizational barriers rather than just a lack of psychological safety. It’s aimed at leaders and managers who rely on surveys or “tell your manager” approaches and want practical ways to remove specific obstacles to employee voice, especially when issues involve managers or root-cause org design problems.

title: TBM 215: Shallow vs. Deep Alignment
questions: How do we distinguish shallow alignment from deep alignment in our team?
What does it mean to treat alignment as a continuous system rather than a checkbox?
Why do OKRs and action items fail to guarantee true alignment?
How can we create psychological safety to support disagreement and tension during alignment?
search_terms: how to maintain alignment over time
summary: This post argues that organizational alignment is a continuous, adaptive process of building shared purpose and understanding, not a one-time achievement or slide-ready consensus. It contrasts shallow alignment (low-tension, artifact-driven) with deep alignment (messy, tension-including, requiring psychological safety), offering guidance for teams that rely too heavily on OKRs and other proxies. It is aimed at leaders and managers who want more resilient, creative alignment practices.

title: TBM 206: When is RACI the Right Tool?
questions: When is RACI the right tool for a team or project?
What team dynamics (trust, norms, psychological safety, incentives) indicate RACI will help?
How do dependencies and negative externalities between teams affect the need for RACI?
When should leaders avoid RACI and use other mechanisms instead?
summary: The post outlines practical situations where a RACI (responsibility assignment) matrix is likely to improve collaboration by clarifying roles and ownership. It is aimed at leaders and project managers dealing with unstable teams, low trust, unclear mandates, misaligned incentives, and complex dependencies. It also implicitly contrasts RACI with alternatives, suggesting RACI is most appropriate when other support for team resilience and alignment is limited.

title: TBM 2/52: "High Standards"
questions: What enables a company to consistently hold itself to high standards?
Is uncompromising excellence driven more by founder charisma or by competence and self-awareness?
How do leaders balance conviction with humility to pivot when needed?
Why does coherence between leadership actions and culture matter more than specific tactics?
summary: The post explores what makes “high standards” stick in companies, arguing that it’s not enough for leaders to be zealous and detail-oriented without real competence, humility, and self-awareness. It contrasts founder CEO archetypes with more general leadership qualities and emphasizes congruence/coherence—whether words match actions and whether leadership practices align with the surrounding culture. It’s aimed at readers thinking about leadership effectiveness, culture building, and how to operationalize excellence.

title: TBM 11/52: Healthy Goals
questions: How can you tell whether your goal-setting framework is helping or harming your team?
What signals show that goals are supporting psychological safety and real accountability?
How do you design goals to encourage sustainability, focus, and aligned autonomy?
What should you do when team members can’t admit they need help or that goals were too hard?
search_terms: how to make goals work for you
summary: The post argues that goal-setting frameworks only work when they create a safe, motivating environment that supports sustainability, focus, and aligned autonomy. It’s aimed at managers and team leads who want to use goals for learning and progress rather than for blame, crunch-time, or restricting collaboration. The key test is whether team members can speak honestly when they miss goals and ask for help, tools, or process changes.

title: TBM 36/53:  Self-similarity and Manager/Leader Accountability (for Cross-functional Teams)
questions: Who should be accountable for the success and health of a cross-functional team?
What happens when cross-functional teams exist structurally but leaders and middle managers remain functional?
How do misaligned incentives between product and engineering show up inside cross-functional teams?
What does it take to make middle management cross-functional, not just the teams?
search_terms: proxy trust backlogs handoffs rigid roles
summary: The post argues that cross-functional teams succeed only when the managers above them are also cross-functional and collaboratively accountable. It explains how distant or adversarial functional managers can cause teams to devolve into siloed “three teams,” driven by misaligned incentives and lack of sustaining sponsorship. Intended for leaders and practitioners designing operating models for product, design, and engineering collaboration.
